g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Validation and Concurrency Control Techniques in Database Management Systems.
Concurrent database operations can cause data inconsistencies without proper control mechanisms.
g.text: • Problems: Lost Updates, Uncommitted Data, Inconsistent Retrievals
• Goal: Maintain data integrity while maximizing concurrency

g.data-narration: Concurrency control techniques are classified into pessimistic and optimistic approaches based on conflict handling.
Optimistic techniques allow transactions to execute freely and validate only at commit, avoiding deadlocks entirely.
Validation-based control operates in three distinct phases: Read, Validation, and Write phases.
In the Read Phase, transactions read data into their private workspace, isolated from uncommitted changes.
g.text: • Transaction reads data from database into private workspace
• All reads are performed against local copies
• Isolation: Cannot see uncommitted changes from other transactions

g.data-narration: The Validation Phase checks if committing would violate serializability by examining read-write conflicts.
g.text: • System performs validation when transaction attempts to commit
• Checks for conflicts with committed transactions since validation began
• If validation fails: transaction is rolled back and restarted

g.data-narration: In the Write Phase, validated local changes are copied to the actual database, making them visible to all.
g.text: • If validation succeeds, local changes are written to database
• Changes become visible to all concurrent transactions

g.data-narration: The validation test ensures serializability through three conditions checking read-write phase overlaps.
g.text: For Ti to validate against Tj:
Ti reads before Tj writes, no conflict
Tj completes read before Ti completes read

g.data-narration: Timestamp-based control assigns unique timestamps to transactions for ordering, ensuring conflict serializability.
g.text: • Each transaction receives a timestamp when it begins
• Read/Write operations allowed based on timestamp ordering

g.data-narration: The timestamp ordering protocol allows reads and writes only when timestamps satisfy specific conditions.
g.text: Ti can read X only if TS(Ti) > write_timestamp(X)
Ti can write X only if TS(Ti) > read_timestamp(X)

g.data-narration: Thomas's Write Rule allows certain writes to be ignored rather than rejected, improving concurrency.
g.text: • A modification to basic timestamp ordering protocol
• Allows certain writes to be IGNORED rather than rejected
• Improves concurrency while maintaining conflict serializability
Basic: REJECT write if TS < write_ts

g.data-narration: Consider two transactions: T1 adds 50 to A, T2 adds 100 to A, both reading initial value 100.
g.text: Conflict detected: both read same initial value

g.data-narration: With timestamps, T1(10) reading X with write_timestamp(X)=20 gets rejected because 10 < 20.
Validation-based techniques offer advantages: no deadlocks possible, high concurrency, better throughput for reads.
g.text: No deadlocks can occur (no locks during execution)

g.data-narration: However, validation-based techniques may cause overhead from transaction aborts in high-conflict scenarios.
g.text: Transaction aborts cause significant overhead in high-conflict scenarios
Validation phase can become a bottleneck
Wasted work when transactions frequently rollback

g.data-narration: In summary: validation-based control uses three phases, timestamps order transactions, and Thomas's rule improves concurrency.
g.text: • Validation-based control: Read → Validation → Write phases
• Timestamp ordering ensures conflict serializability without locks
• Thomas's Write Rule improves concurrency by ignoring certain writes
• No deadlocks possible in validation-based (optimistic) techniques
• Optimistic techniques best for low-conflict, read-heavy environments
